sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Post-submersion refers to survival following immersion with actual or potential respiratory compromise. Presentation ranges from an apparently well patient with a submersion history to severe hypoxia, aspiration, pulmonary oedema, hypothermia, or cardiac arrest. Airway management and ventilation take priority.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Altered level of consciousness, confusion, agitation, or seizure
- Dysrhythmias, hypotension, or cardiac arrest
- Dyspnoea, non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, aspiration, ARDS, or respiratory arrest
- Spinal injury, vomiting, hypothermia, or associated diving injury

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Hypoxia and aspiration are the main immediate threats after submersion
- Vomiting and regurgitation are common; do not attempt to empty the stomach by external pressure
- Consciousness is not the same as recovery and delayed deterioration can occur
- Consider hypothermia and possible spinal injury, but do not delay rescue, airway management, or ventilation for spinal precautions alone

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Remove the patient from the water as quickly and safely as possible
- Prioritise airway opening and ventilation; suction and roll only as needed to clear vomit or debris
- Manage cardiac arrest under the appropriate arrest guideline
- Remove wet clothing, keep the patient warm, and administer oxygen for respiratory compromise or hypoxia
- Record vital signs including BGL and temperature, and complete a focused secondary survey
- Apply spinal-motion restriction only when mechanism, examination, or GCS supports it
- Transport all post-submersion patients for hospital assessment

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Establish IV or IO access if indicated
- Escalate to CPAP, IPPV, or PEEP-supported ventilation within scope for persistent respiratory compromise or hypoxia
- Consider fluids only when clinically indicated and avoid excessive volumes
- Consider gastric decompression only in appropriately advanced, intubated, or ventilated patients within scope
- Provide analgesia and antiemetic therapy within scope
- Escalate early for respiratory compromise, persistent hypoxia, hypothermia, or associated trauma

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- All post-submersion patients require hospital assessment because pulmonary oedema, aspiration-related complications, and delayed respiratory decline may develop after apparent initial recovery
- Consider precipitating causes such as intoxication, seizure, stroke, acute coronary syndrome, trauma, or diving illness
- Document submersion duration if known, rescue sequence, initial respiratory status, temperature, and oxygen or ventilation requirements
